title: Guide to the Java ArrayList
body:
`ArrayList<E>` is a resizable-array implementation of the `List` interface. It stores elements in insertion order and allows duplicates.

Key operations:
- `add(e)` ‚Äî append; `add(index, e)` ‚Äî insert
- `get(index)` ‚Äî retrieve; `set(index, e)` ‚Äî replace
- `remove(index)` or `remove(Object)` ‚Äî delete
- `size()` ‚Äî number of elements
- `contains(e)` ‚Äî membership test (linear search)
- `indexOf(e)` ‚Äî first occurrence index
- `clear()` ‚Äî remove all elements
- `subList(from, to)` ‚Äî view of a range

Performance:
- `get/set`: O(1)
- `add` at end: amortized O(1)
- `add/remove` at middle: O(n)

Prefer `ArrayList` for random access; `LinkedList` for frequent insertions/deletions at arbitrary positions. Use `Collections.unmodifiableList()` or `List.of()` for immutable lists.

examples.label: ArrayList basics

title: A Guide to the Java LinkedList
body:
`LinkedList<E>` is a doubly-linked list implementing both `List` and `Deque`. Each element is a node holding the value plus references to the previous and next nodes.

Unique operations (via `Deque`):
- `addFirst(e)` / `addLast(e)` ‚Äî O(1) head/tail insertion
- `removeFirst()` / `removeLast()` ‚Äî O(1) head/tail removal
- `peekFirst()` / `peekLast()` ‚Äî inspect without removing
- `poll()` ‚Äî retrieves and removes the head

Performance:
- `add/remove` at head or tail: O(1)
- `get(index)`: O(n) ‚Äî must traverse from head

Use `LinkedList` as a **queue** (`add`/`poll`), **stack** (`push`/`pop`), or **deque**. Use `ArrayDeque` for pure queue/stack needs ‚Äî it is faster and uses less memory.

examples.label: LinkedList as a queue

title: A Guide to HashSet in Java
body:
`HashSet<E>` is an unordered collection of unique elements backed by a `HashMap`. It provides O(1) average time for `add`, `remove`, and `contains`.

Key characteristics:
- No duplicate elements (determined by `equals()` and `hashCode()`)
- No guaranteed iteration order
- Allows one `null` element
- Not thread-safe

Common operations:
- `add(e)` ‚Äî adds element; returns `false` if already present
- `remove(e)` ‚Äî removes element
- `contains(e)` ‚Äî membership test
- `size()`, `isEmpty()`, `clear()`
- `addAll(collection)` ‚Äî union
- `retainAll(collection)` ‚Äî intersection
- `removeAll(collection)` ‚Äî difference

Use `LinkedHashSet` to maintain insertion order, or `TreeSet` for sorted order.

examples.label: HashSet operations

title: A Guide to Java HashMap
body:
`HashMap<K,V>` stores key-value pairs with O(1) average time for `put`, `get`, and `remove`. Keys must have correct `equals()` and `hashCode()` implementations.

Key characteristics:
- Keys are unique; values can be duplicated
- Allows one `null` key and multiple `null` values
- Iteration order is not guaranteed
- Not thread-safe (use `ConcurrentHashMap` for concurrent access)

Useful methods:
- `put(k, v)`, `get(k)`, `remove(k)`, `containsKey(k)`, `containsValue(v)`
- `getOrDefault(k, default)` ‚Äî safe get
- `putIfAbsent(k, v)` ‚Äî only puts if key absent
- `computeIfAbsent(k, fn)` ‚Äî compute and store if absent
- `merge(k, v, fn)` ‚Äî update existing value
- `forEach((k, v) -> ...)` ‚Äî iterate entries
- `entrySet()`, `keySet()`, `values()` ‚Äî views

examples.label: Word frequency counter

title: Initialize a HashMap in Java
body:
There are several ways to initialize a `HashMap` in Java:

**1. Empty then `put`:**
```java
Map<String, Integer> map = new HashMap<>();
map.put("a", 1);
```

**2. Copy constructor from another map:**
```java
Map<String, Integer> copy = new HashMap<>(existingMap);
```

**3. `Map.of()` (Java 9+) ‚Äî immutable, up to 10 entries:**
```java
Map<String, Integer> map = Map.of("a", 1, "b", 2);
```

**4. `Map.ofEntries()` ‚Äî immutable, any number:**
```java
Map<String, Integer> map = Map.ofEntries(
    Map.entry("a", 1),
    Map.entry("b", 2)
);
```

**5. `Map.copyOf()` ‚Äî immutable copy:**
```java
Map<String, Integer> immutable = Map.copyOf(mutableMap);
```

`Map.of()` and `Map.ofEntries()` throw `NullPointerException` for null keys or values and `IllegalArgumentException` for duplicate keys.

examples.label: Map.ofEntries initialization

title: A Guide to Iterator in Java
body:
`Iterator<E>` provides a standard way to traverse a collection and safely remove elements during iteration.

```java
Iterator<String> it = list.iterator();
while (it.hasNext()) {
    String s = it.next();
    if (s.isEmpty()) it.remove(); // safe removal
}
```

Key methods:
- `hasNext()` ‚Äî returns `true` if more elements remain
- `next()` ‚Äî returns the next element and advances the cursor
- `remove()` ‚Äî removes the last element returned by `next()` (optional operation)

**`ListIterator<E>`** extends `Iterator` with:
- `hasPrevious()`, `previous()` ‚Äî bidirectional traversal
- `set(e)` ‚Äî replace current element
- `add(e)` ‚Äî insert during iteration

Modifying a collection while iterating (without using `Iterator.remove()`) causes `ConcurrentModificationException`. Java 8+ alternatives: `removeIf()`, streams.

examples.label: Remove with iterator

title: Converting Between an Array and a List in Java
body:
Converting between arrays and lists is a common operation.

**Array ‚Üí List:**
- `Arrays.asList(arr)` ‚Äî fixed-size list (cannot add/remove, but can set)
- `new ArrayList<>(Arrays.asList(arr))` ‚Äî mutable list
- `List.of(elements)` ‚Äî immutable list (Java 9+)
- `Arrays.stream(arr).collect(Collectors.toList())` ‚Äî via streams

**List ‚Üí Array:**
- `list.toArray()` ‚Äî returns `Object[]`
- `list.toArray(new String[0])` ‚Äî returns typed array (preferred)
- `list.stream().mapToInt(Integer::intValue).toArray()` ‚Äî for primitive arrays

**Important:** `Arrays.asList()` wraps an existing array ‚Äî changes to the list are reflected in the array and vice versa. Primitive arrays (`int[]`) cannot be directly used with `Arrays.asList()` ‚Äî use `Integer[]` instead.

examples.label: Array ‚Üî List conversions
